author: @jco-physics
text:
Guys, what we're seeing is the voltage across the capacitor it's not the atmospheric voltage, what we are seeing is the incremental increase in voltage of the cap each times the circuit is switched - this is not the ambient voltage of the atmosphere, this is the resultant voltage of the cap after many cycles. I slowed down the video the Voltage and counted the voltage increments, they averaged about 17 mV per circuit switching. (These figures are rough estimates). 
It ranged from 169mV (at 4:07) to 941 mV (at 4:31) in total voltage spread. That's about 45 switches in 25 seconds with a total increase of about 772 mV, which is about 0.03088 Volts/second. That means it would take about 9 hours to charge up to a 1000 Volts. 
Now a 1000 Volts sounds it would make a lot of energy-right? The energy W measured in Joules that is stored in a capacitor is W = 1/2 C V^2 (where C is capacitance). Capacitance for different capacitors is often given in units of microFarads mF or nanoFarads nF (sorry I can't use Greek symbols here), so let's assume a high frequency capacitor of 1000 V and .33 uF W = 1/2 C V^2 = 0.5 * .33 * 10^-6 * 10^6 = 0.164 Joules That's not a lot of energy. 
So, the working voltage V(w) of this device is about 17 mV, with an energy of about 0.163 Joules, but what we need is the current 'I' to calculate the power P = V(w) * I , so we can figure out the Amp-Hours that could be generated. 
Perhaps-like me-you saw 941mV and thought 'Wow, that's significant!' , but in reality it's only generating 17 mV. This won't run my desktop computer on 20V, that would require over 1000 of these antennas that to work. Imagine if everyone had a 1000 antennas in their backyard they would block out the sun and more importantly the rotation of the Earth would slow down because we had increased the effective angular momentum of Yorkshire. 
Now yes, if left to it's own devices this (I'm calling it) "Atmospheric Ram Pump" might charge up your phone battery overnight, and I think this has potential (pun intended) for off grid battery generators like when you're hiking or sailing, but it's clearly not going to compete with a nuclear fusion generator.

replies.author: @totherarf
replies.text:
@Mary-me9so Sorry if this gets too long ..... but Everything right down to Atoms has a resonant frequency!

To simplify it, think of the old skipping games kids used to play. You had a long rope held at eather end and swung round in an arc. Kids would then run into the middle and jump over the swinging rope. Then some smart kid would suddenly swing the rope faster and a new equilibrium would be set up ..... but the rope would no longer be in a simple arc, it would be motionless in the middle and each half of the rope would swing in opposition to the other. That is Harmonics!
Everything has this phenomena ..... not always in physical motion. If you look at the propagation of an Electromagnetic wave you will be taught that there are two components, the Electrical and the Magnetic (hence the name). It starts off with say the electric field being formed, exactly like the skipping rope. As the electric field collapses to zero it has a magnetic component that is at right angles to it along the axis of propagation. 
In a vacuum the speed of propagation is the same as light (light is a form of Electromagnetic wave) but when it passes through something that speed slows (like light going through glass) .
So the frequency has natural balance points where the effects are amplified and others where it is reduced dependant on the medium it is passing through. That is why you can get a glass to "ring" when you rub it right, or even shatter if you are a specially gifted singer. 
So, given there are resonant frequencies the Earth as a whole will have them. I was wondering if the frequency of switching (it would almost certainly need an electronic switch to be both stable and quick enough) would affect the rate of charge.
Indeed there is an electrical variant of this where Capacitors, Inductors and Resistors of specific value in relationship to each other can cause resonance. 

Sorry if my explanation is not up to Rob's standards of getting complex ideas across simply and concisely!
